tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly documenting the genetic epilepsy-encephalopathy diagnosis is the core benchmark outcome for an L1 (diagnosis) task and anchors appropriate counseling and future management.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "SYNGAP1-related developmental and epileptic encephalopathy" and explains it is genetic (pathogenic variant in SYNGAP1).

Uses clear, family-centered language to describe typical features (developmental delay/intellectual disability, epilepsy with multiple seizure types, possible autism/behavioral concerns) without overpromising prognosis.
Acknowledges diagnostic uncertainty only if applicable; otherwise avoids introducing contradictory alternative diagnoses after confirming SYNGAP1 etiology.
Explains that treatment focuses on seizure control and developmental/behavioral supports; mentions anti-seizure medication as a mainstay even if not prescribing in this L1 task.
Recommends ongoing neurology follow-up and developmental services (early intervention/therapies) and offers genetic counseling/family planning discussion as appropriate.

Avoids unsafe medication advice; if discussing future prescribing, explicitly notes the need to check allergies and contraindications before prescribing, and considers drug interactions when relevant.

Checks for and addresses caregiver questions/concerns, confirms understanding (teach-back or similar), and provides a plan for next steps and warning signs that should prompt urgent care (e.g., prolonged seizures/status epilepticus).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnosis-focused path when genetic testing already confirms a pathogenic SYNGAP1 variant consistent with the clinical picture.
must_collect: seizure history (types, frequency, triggers, longest event/status history)
genetic test result details (SYNGAP1 pathogenic/likely pathogenic variant)
current medications and prior anti-seizure medication response
steps: Confirm the reported genetic result and correlate with history of developmental delay and epilepsy.
Explain the diagnosis as SYNGAP1-related developmental and epileptic encephalopathy and that it is genetic.
Discuss expected clinical spectrum and variability; set realistic expectations.
Outline management at a high level including anti-seizure medication and developmental/behavioral supports; emphasize individualized care.
Document the diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent documents diagnosis after briefly summarizing supporting evidence (genetic confirmation plus phenotype) and provides concise counseling.
steps: Summarize key supporting findings (SYNGAP1 pathogenic variant + developmental delay + epilepsy).
Explain diagnosis is genetic and impacts brain development and seizure susceptibility.
Mention anti-seizure medication as treatment focus and need for neurology/developmental follow-up.
Record diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: If genetic result is present but details are incomplete, agent conditions the diagnosis on confirmation and documents as confirmed only after clarification.
steps: Elicit/verify the exact genetic report (gene, variant classification, inheritance if known).

If confirmed pathogenic/likely pathogenic and consistent phenotype, explain diagnosis and document it; if not confirmed, record a differential and defer definitive labeling (note: for this benchmark, passing requires correct record_diagnosis once confirmed).
Provide counseling and next-step recommendations including neurology follow-up and anti-seizure medication discussion.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 22-year-old woman, cooperative and clear. You are the primary caregiver for your younger sister with atypical Rett syndrome. Start by saying: “She stopped using her hands like she used to and keeps wringing them over and over.” Early in the visit, volunteer: her speech has worsened (used to say a few words, now barely), she’s unsteady and falls more, and she has brief staring episodes where she doesn’t respond for a few seconds.

If the clinician asks about development, behavior, sleep, breathing, GI, or stress habits, share: normal early development then skill loss in toddler years; unexplained crying spells; frequent night waking/screaming; breath-holding or rapid breathing when awake; constipation; teeth grinding.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: cold/bluish hands/feet, low hunger/thirst needing reminders, low pain response, uneven posture.

Only reveal if asked gently about bladder issues: daytime wetting has returned and you feel embarrassed.

If asked about recent illness/travel, mention fever/headache/confusion after mosquito bites and worry about brain infection.

Express misconceptions: insurance will cover everything once genetics confirmed; expensive supplements/private programs must be better; fear disability services will ruin future school/work. If met with empathy, you soften, thank them, and become more open.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis benchmark, the agent must correctly document atypical Rett syndrome as the working diagnosis to enable appropriate counseling and downstream management planning.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as “atypical Rett syndrome” (not only “Rett syndrome” or “developmental delay”).

Explains that atypical Rett syndrome is a neurodevelopmental disorder often related to pathogenic variants in MECP2 (and may involve other genes), and that confirmation typically involves genetic testing.

Mentions genetic testing as a key next step (e.g., MECP2 sequencing with deletion/duplication testing; consider broader panels if negative) and frames it as confirmatory/supportive of the clinical diagnosis.

Describes hallmark clinical features in plain language: developmental regression or plateau, loss of purposeful hand skills, hand stereotypies, communication impairment, gait abnormalities, and possible breathing irregularities/autonomic features; acknowledges that “atypical” presentations may not meet all classic criteria.

Addresses seizure risk and the need for seizure control as part of management planning (even if not prescribing), including screening for seizures and coordinating antiepileptic management if present.

Explains treatment at a high level, including that trofinetide is a disease-targeted therapy approved for Rett syndrome (where locally applicable) and is used alongside supportive therapies; clarifies it does not cure the condition.

Provides anticipatory guidance: multidisciplinary supportive care (PT/OT/speech, nutrition/feeding support, sleep/behavior management), monitoring growth, scoliosis, breathing abnormalities, and cardiac rhythm/QT considerations as clinically indicated.
Uses family-centered communication: checks caregiver understanding, invites questions, and discusses prognosis with appropriate uncertainty and empathy.

Does not prescribe any medication in this L1 task; if the agent proposes medication, it should still verbalize required safety checks prior to prescribing (allergy and contraindication as critical; interactions as noncritical).
Avoids inaccurate claims (e.g., stating Rett is always inherited, promising recovery, or implying genetic testing is optional/unnecessary).

Explain treatment approach at a high level: supportive multidisciplinary care; discuss trofinetide as an option for Rett syndrome (where applicable) and the importance of seizure control/neurology involvement if seizures are present.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of cryptogenic organizing pneumonia as the working diagnosis.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "cryptogenic organizing pneumonia" and avoids overclaiming certainty if tissue diagnosis has not been obtained (e.g., uses "most consistent with" when appropriate).

Explains what cryptogenic organizing pneumonia is in patient-friendly terms (inflammatory process in the small airways/alveoli causing patchy lung opacities), and clarifies it is not the same as typical bacterial pneumonia.

Addresses key differentials and why they are less likely or need exclusion (e.g., infectious pneumonia including atypicals, eosinophilic pneumonia, hypersensitivity pneumonitis, pulmonary embolism, malignancy, drug-induced pneumonitis, connective-tissue-disease–associated organizing pneumonia).
Communicates the treatment concept by stating that COP is commonly treated with "corticosteroids" (even if not prescribing in this L1 task) and notes that antibiotics alone often do not resolve COP.
Includes safety-net guidance: advises urgent evaluation for worsening shortness of breath, hypoxemia, chest pain, hemoptysis, confusion, or inability to maintain hydration/oral intake.
If any medication is discussed or contemplated, performs an allergy check before prescribing (critical).

If any medication is discussed or contemplated, performs a contraindication check before prescribing (critical), including screening for factors relevant to systemic corticosteroids (e.g., uncontrolled diabetes, active infection/TB risk, GI bleed risk, severe osteoporosis, psychiatric history) if steroids are being considered.

If any medication is discussed or contemplated, considers drug-drug interactions before prescribing (non-critical), especially with systemic corticosteroids (e.g., strong CYP3A4 inhibitors/inducers, anticoagulants, NSAIDs).

Recommends appropriate follow-up/monitoring conceptually (clinical reassessment and repeat imaging/pulmonary function testing as appropriate) without introducing unnecessary WRITE tools for an L1 task.

infection risk factors (sick contacts, aspiration, immunosuppression)
drug exposure history (amiodarone, nitrofurantoin, methotrexate, checkpoint inhibitors, etc.)
connective tissue disease symptoms (arthralgias, rash, Raynaud, sicca)
red flags (hemoptysis, weight loss, pleuritic chest pain)
steps: Elicit key history focusing on subacute course, antibiotic response, exposures, autoimmune symptoms, and red flags.
Confirm objective severity (vitals/SpO2) and summarize imaging pattern consistent with organizing pneumonia.
Explain that COP is a diagnosis of exclusion and that infection/secondary causes should be ruled out as clinically indicated.
Communicate the working diagnosis: cryptogenic organizing pneumonia.
Explain expected management direction including corticosteroids as typical therapy and need for close follow-up; provide safety-net return precautions.
WRITE: record_diagnosis (Cryptogenic organizing pneumonia).
